name: The ARM Revolution
description: When Apple launched the iPhone in 2007 on an ARM Cortex-A8 derivative, it redefined what a mobile CPU had to do: render a full WebKit browser, play H.264 video, and run rich applications, all from a 1,200 mAh battery. Single-core frequency scaling had hit a thermal wall around 1 GHz, so designers squeezed every instruction-per-clock out of out-of-order pipelines and wider SIMD units. The tension was ISA efficiency: x86's legacy baggage cost power that ARM's clean RISC load-store architecture avoided.
